archetypes.name: The In-Role AI Upskiller
archetypes.background: Working analytics-adjacent professional (2-13 years) — business analysts, data analysts, finance/consulting staff at service MNCs and fintechs — already fluent in Excel, SQL, Power BI, earning roughly 4.5-18+ LPA. They watched AI arrive inside their own job (Copilot rollouts, agent projects) and came pre-researched, often via LinkedIn or after comparing self-paced alternatives.
archetypes.goals: Bolt GenAI and agentic AI onto an existing analytics career: automate manual reporting and dashboards, jump from a service company to a product company, or unlock a managerial/senior role and CTC hike without leaving their domain.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: Whether the course is deep enough (or conversely too basic) for someone already in the field — and whether their 'basic Python' will survive the ML/AI modules.
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Self-funded from their own salary and decide solo; the only gates are logistics — night-shift class clash, EMI interest terms, occasional employer reimbursement approval, or one quick family check. Once those clear, many pay within the session.
archetypes.languageTexture: Confident, structured English with corporate vocabulary and low filler; asks precise, comparative questions before committing. Examples: 'instead of working manually, I may go and check with autonomous', 'What is the ROI on this course from the previous batches?'
typicalQuestions: Is the neural networks and deep learning section theoretical or practical application?
Will my basic Python be enough to keep up with the advanced ML modules?
I work a 2 PM to 11 PM shift — how will I manage the live classes?
EMI is zero cost or they have to make an interest?
What will be the contribution of IIM here — whose faculty actually teaches?
Will I be able to build my own AI agents for my daily work after this?
For placements, from which week or month do they start?

archetypes.name: The Automation-Scared Switcher
archetypes.background: 2-10 years grinding in operations, sales, support, BPO, procurement, retail or core engineering — loan processing, warehouse management, pharma sales, IT support, quality engineering — usually a tier-2/3 native working in a metro on 3-8 LPA, often 6-day weeks.
archetypes.goals: Escape into a business/data analyst role before AI eats their job; concrete salary-doubling targets (8 LPA to 15 LPA) and the security of a 'future-proof' skill; placement support is the headline draw.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: Job displacement plus self-doubt — '80% of my work is done by generative AI already' on one side, 'I'm not technically that much good, actually' on the other.
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Nominally self-funded but cash-flow fragile: payment waits for the 5th-of-month salary credit, recent big purchases, or a quick check with a brother or parents. They ask for 'a day or two', accept a WhatsApp payment-link deadline, and most never come back.
archetypes.languageTexture: Hinglish with polite hedging and heavy fillers ('okay okay', 'like', 'actually'); broken-to-moderate English, short answers, low assertiveness. Examples: 'I'm a non-technical guy, so will it be a challenge to me learn?', 'the operations department I am working in is going to completely vanish as AI is taking our jobs'.
typicalQuestions: Is it placement assistance or 100% placement guarantee?
I am from a non-technical background — will I be able to grasp the coding part?
EMI me interest lagega ya zero cost hai?
Can I pay after my salary credits on the 5th?
What package can I expect after completing the course?
Classes office time me padti hain — recording mil jayegi na?
Is this course actually useful for someone from my field or not?

archetypes.name: The Skeptical Senior Evaluator
archetypes.background: 8-25 years of experience — bank risk managers, Navy officer near retirement, operations heads, agency strategy leads, 13-year single-employer analysts — many already using ChatGPT, Claude or Copilot at work and earning 12-20+ LPA.
archetypes.goals: Hands-on, immediately applicable AI capability for their current role or a pre-retirement reinvention; they explicitly do not care about the certificate — 'I'm not looking just for the certification part, I want something hands-on'.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: Paying for six months of content they already know, or skills their regulated employer will never let them deploy — wasted time matters more than the fee.
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Fully self-funded, decide alone, and refuse same-day closes on principle: 'I need to compare the other parameters... and then take a decision.' Urgency tactics visibly backfire; they demand batch data, alumni outcomes and a week to think. Zero converted in-session in this corpus.
archetypes.languageTexture: Precise, formal English with domain jargon, calm but firm pushback; treats the counsellor as a vendor to be audited. Examples: 'boss, to be very honest, in my scope of work I don't think I need to build any agentic AI', 'prompt engineering itself is a different certification altogether, right? What portion will you cover?'
typicalQuestions: What is the ROI on this course from your previous batches?
How many students in the last batch were from non-technical backgrounds — do you have that data?
If the AI trend changes rapidly, do the modules also change?
Will these tools even be usable in a regulated bank environment like mine?
Is the depth of prompt engineering and LLM coverage enough, or is it a surface tour?
Why must I decide today? I need a week to consult my colleagues.
At my age and seniority, does switching profession even make sense?

archetypes.name: The Reel-Struck Parent-Gated Fresher
archetypes.background: 17-20 years old, just out of 12th or in first/second year at a tier-2/3 college; discovered the programme on Instagram reels and often registered without reading details — some did not know it was paid. Fathers are government employees, shopkeepers, mill owners; parents usually unaware of the application.
archetypes.goals: Vague future-proofing — 'AI is the future' — productive use of a pre-college gap or semester holiday; a few literally signed up out of boredom or curiosity after an ad.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: The fee revelation and the conversation with parents that must follow; underneath it, a zero technical base and no formed career direction.
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Father (sometimes mother) is the absolute financial gatekeeper. Conversions in this corpus happened almost only when the counsellor got a parent on the call mid-session and walked them through the EMI; 'I will discuss with my parents' otherwise ends it.
archetypes.languageTexture: Hesitant, deferential 'Yes sir / Yes ma'am' loops, broken Hinglish, trailing sentences and restarts when nervous. Examples: 'I was scrolling reels and watched about it', 'I literally have to ask my parents once again because I have to discuss about the fees I guess'.
typicalQuestions: Sir, is this course free or do we have to pay?
Can I confirm tomorrow after my father comes home from the shop?
Is there any scholarship test or discount for students?
Will the classes clash with my college timing and exams?
Mummy se pooch ke bata doon, abhi woh ghar pe nahi hain?
Can I attend from my phone, I don't have a laptop?
What exactly is business analytics, sir?

archetypes.name: The Credential-Stacking Campus Achiever
archetypes.background: Articulate students at strong institutions (IIT, IISER, NIT, DU, BHU, private CS/BBA programmes) or high-achieving fresh graduates heading for MBA or masters abroad; resumes already carry internships, MUNs, hackathons, small ventures, even 90%+ boards.
archetypes.goals: Real industry projects plus an IIM-branded certificate to beat campus-placement competition, strengthen MBA/MS applications, or arm a startup ambition; they want an edge over peers, not rescue.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: Whether the certificate is 'industrially valid' — will it actually convert to internships, PPOs and product-company offers, or is it just another paper credential next to IIT/IIM peers?
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Self-driven and pre-researched; parents fund but treat it as the student's call — a quick mid-call mute-and-ask usually returns a yes, and several paid the seat-booking amount in-session. Second-highest conversion cluster.
archetypes.languageTexture: Fluent, confident English with structured multi-part questions and almost no Hinglish; comfortable challenging the pitch. Examples: 'what do your top performing students usually do differently in this course?', 'I have projects, but not the real-time work'.
typicalQuestions: How industrially valid is this certificate — will it smoothly get me an internship or a PPO?
What do your top-performing students do differently?
Will I get real-time industry projects, not just coursework?
Will I be given the tools to practice on or do I have to buy the subscriptions?
Is the 50,000 plus GST fixed, or do you provide any discount?
How does this compare to what my IIT and IIM peers are getting on campus?
Can you share a PDF or brochure so my father can review it tonight?

archetypes.name: The Family-Business Modernizer
archetypes.background: Owners and heirs of family businesses — pharma wholesale, real estate development, garment export, electrical trading, coaching institutes, event management, D2C food brands — ranging from a college drop-out who joined the trade to a 41-year-old MBA founder with US/UK clients; tier-2/3 heavy.
archetypes.goals: Apply analytics and AI directly to their own business: demand forecasting, inventory, buyer prediction, digitising a mandi-era operation, scaling e-commerce. A job placement is at most a backup, not the point.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: Whether classroom tools will actually plug into their real systems and trade ('will I be able to use it into my systems also, like Shopify?'), compounded by brutal time poverty — 6-7 day working weeks.
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Self- or family-funded from business income; established founders decide fast, sometimes after one spousal check, and even ask about GST input credit on the fee. A slow business cycle stalls the rest — 'for the last six-seven months not everything is going well'.
archetypes.languageTexture: Practical, direct, business-jargon Hinglish; confident about their trade, humble about tech. Examples: 'My whole social media is managed by AI... every day I do outreach through emails, that is also automated', 'I want to do basic analytics of my business so that I no need to take consultant from another's'.
typicalQuestions: Can I apply this directly to my own business, or is it only job-oriented?
If I invest six months, will I actually be capable of creating an AI agent?
I am free only on Sundays — can I still manage the course?
Can I take GST input credit on the fee?
Is there a demo class before I pay this much?
Business is slow right now — can I join the next batch instead?
Will this help me digitalize and predict outcomes in our family business?

archetypes.name: The Exam-Track Backup Seeker
archetypes.background: 2-5 years sunk into UPSC, NEET or CA pipelines — humanities and commerce backgrounds, repeated attempts, gap years — some with a live attempt still pending (PCS in 45 days, CA finals, NEET retake). Financially dependent and emotionally invested in the exam identity.
archetypes.goals: A non-coding plan B that monetises their 'analytical' exam-prep skills without openly admitting the dream is over; the CA-track variant wants AI purely as a differentiator — 'I want to stand out between my peers'.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: Betting on two horses: can they prepare for the exam and do the course, and what happens to the non-refundable fee if they actually clear; plus whether gap years have already poisoned their job prospects.
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Parents fund and parents defer everything until results — 'they are asking me to let the result come and you just decide later'; in the CA variant the CA father dismisses Python as irrelevant to the path. Zero conversions in this corpus.
archetypes.languageTexture: Measured, sequential, risk-listing Hindi-English; politely interrogates every contingency before moving an inch. Examples: 'how suitable would it be for a non-tech background, according to your records?', 'if fortunately I get to clear, this 4,000 is not refundable, right?'
typicalQuestions: If I clear my exam mid-course, is the seat-booking amount refundable?
Can I genuinely manage this alongside my PCS/CA preparation?
How suitable is it for a humanities person with zero coding, as per your records?
Will my gap years affect the placement outcomes?
Why should I take this when ICAI is adding AI to its own curriculum?
How long does your placement support last after the course?

archetypes.name: The Career-Break Returner
archetypes.background: Women with solid prior careers — banking, biotech research, sales leadership, software development — now on 1-6 year breaks for maternity, medical treatment or family care; a PhD and an ex-developer with two daughters sit in this cluster alongside ex-bankers.
archetypes.goals: A credible bridge back into the workforce: analytics roles that are ideally work-from-home compatible and respect childcare constraints; leverage the old domain instead of starting from zero.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: The gap itself — 'I should not go through this course and then land up a job which is totally offline' — plus fear of the technical modules with no coding past.
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Husband or an earning sibling is the financial co-signer and is never available during the call — 'once I will talk to my sister once again, then I will confirm'; the more senior ones are assertive and flatly refuse urgency: 'payment is not one-two rupees, right? It's 52,000'.
archetypes.languageTexture: Polite, composed, formal English; deferential in tone but firm on needing time and on researching reviews/alumni first. Examples: 'I require some time to discuss with my family', 'I need one day to think about it, sir — I did not know we have to pay today itself'.
typicalQuestions: Will the placement support cover work-from-home roles?
Will my five-six year career gap be a problem in placements?
Can I confirm tonight after discussing with my husband?
Is there any scholarship or concession available?
Can I speak to alumni or see placement reviews before deciding?
Does the full fee have to be paid before the programme starts?

archetypes.name: The Yes-Sir Small-Town Drifter
archetypes.background: Tier-3 and rural students and ex-workers — BCom/BA/MCA from small-town colleges, ex-hotel operations, video annotators — with very limited English and near-zero awareness of what analytics is; several discovered only on the call that the programme costs money.
archetypes.goals: A vague 'job' or 'I want to learn AI' with no articulated path; the counsellor drives the entire conversation and the student mostly assents.
archetypes.coreAnxiety: Comprehension itself — they cannot fully follow the English pitch, fear English-medium classes, and the fee reveal lands as a shock ('please ma'am, please' on learning it is paid).
archetypes.decisionDynamics: Family-funded; the playbook conversion is the father called mid-session to transfer money. It is compliance rather than conviction — they agree verbally when steered hard and silently stall the moment any friction appears.
archetypes.languageTexture: Fragmented, monosyllabic, 'Yes sir / okay ma'am' on loop, with restarts and echo-answers. Examples: 'I'm from a long tail background, I don't know anything about it', 'I can't study too much'.
typicalQuestions: Sir, classes Hindi me hongi ya English me?
Is course se job pakka mil jayegi, sir?
Sir, business analytics actually kya hota hai?
Paise abhi dene padenge kya?
Can I do the course from my phone?
Sir, mere jaise non-tech wale ke liye yeh ho payega?
